data.title: What U.S. Reciprocal Tariffs Mean For The Commodities Sector
data.description:
The U.S. has announced sweeping reciprocal tariffs. Daniel Ghali, Director, Commodities Strategy at TD Securities, breaks down what it could mean for oil, gold and the wider commodities sector.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: This new round of US tariffs weighing on the commodity space today, but as we go further out, are there any signs we should be watching for in those sectors? Joining us now to discuss all that, Daniel Ghali, Director for Commodity Strategy with TD Securities. Daniel, great to have you here.

Daniel Ghali: Yeah, thanks for having me, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: So we got a big piece of news. Obviously, the markets are reacting in a fairly significant way. What does it mean for commodities?

Daniel Ghali: Well, look, I think when something like this happens, markets shoot first, think later. So the information that we do have is that the tariff announcements were more significant than expected. The reprieve that Canada and Mexico did receive so far means that the tariff announcements are even more significant for inflation than they are for growth, but they're certainly significant. It's a significant growth shock as well.

Well, I think the commodities complex is trading completely in line with that. We're seeing broad-based selling activity across pretty much any commodity on your radar except for gold, which is the one outlier there. Within precious metals markets, I would say one of the really big stories over the last few months has been that the threat of tariffs has catalyzed a tidal wave of metal movement from everywhere in the world, but particularly from London, into the US. That relates to the market structure. But within one of the annexes tied to the executive order, they clarified that many commodities that we care about are exempt from these tariffs.

So what we've seen is the spread between US-based bullion contracts and other underlying physical metal completely collapsed over the course of this session. Interestingly, that hasn't had a very big impact on gold prices, but arguably, it may have an impact on other precious metals. You see, one of our thesis has been that these implications were unique for silver markets, where we were inadvertently draining the world's largest pool of metal in London. And the question now is, well, if bullion products are exempt, then will the metal that has already moved into the US flow back out?

And that is still up for debate. We think there're reasons to believe that it will flow back out at a much slower pace than it came in, and in which case you still have upside potential in some of these other precious metals. But the most interesting piece of price action today is certainly that gold seems untouchable.

Greg Bonnell: With gold well over $3,100 an ounce and making all these new highs, I think a lot of investors will look at that and say, given the times we're living in, can it move higher from here?

Daniel Ghali: Well, here's the most interesting piece about gold prices right now -- gold is overbought but under-owned. In contrast to what you might expect by looking simply at a chart of the price, we've actually seen very substantial liquidations from the speculative community over the course of the last months. Initially, this was the macro fund community. That's the group of investors who care about things like the Fed outlook, deficit, so on and so forth. They were holding an extreme position of size heading into the US election.

That's no longer the case today. In fact, today we think they hold less than they did in March of last year, where gold prices were trading at $2,000 an ounce. So really, what's driven those liquidations is unclear. It's possible that it's tied to constraints on risk budgets associated with the decline in equity prices as opposed to something that's specifically tied to gold prices itself. But what we do know is that in this moment of time, Western investors are actually under-positioned in gold. And the biggest buyers of gold over the last several weeks have been out of China. That's been a particularly strong story.

Greg Bonnell: Is this the central bank, or is that buying actually moving outside of the central bank?

Daniel Ghali: Well, the central bank is buying, but the new piece of information is that Chinese life insurance companies, for the first time ever, have been granted permission to purchase gold. That in and of itself -- we think Chinese life insurance companies have announced that they have already purchased some gold since they were allowed to. But we think that the narrative has bred animal spirits which are driving Chinese ETF fund flows. So that's really where the buying activity is coming from, is Chinese ETFs potentially tied to retail investing. But very substantial amounts of gold purchases through that channel.

Greg Bonnell: Interesting stuff there on the metals front. I want to talk to you about oil. When I first saw the move and West Texas Intermediate and Brent this morning, I thought, okay, well, tariffs, worries about global growth. But then I saw the OPEC headlines as well. What is going on with oil right now?

Daniel Ghali: Yeah, oil is under attack on several fronts. It's been our view that there's only a very narrow path to substantially higher oil prices this year. Truthfully, that right tail risk, as you might call it, is fairly fat, fatter than is currently being priced by markets. And it mostly relates to the prospect of increasing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.

But for the time being, the combination of a growth shock, a decrease in supply risk premia tied to energy products being exempt from the tariffs, and OPEC announcing that they are bringing barrels back to the market at a faster rate than expected, this is an attack on several fronts on the oil price.

Original Post

data.title: Telix Pharmaceuticals: Building A Radiopharma Powerhouse
data.description:
Telix Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:TLX) is an interesting case study of execution within a dynamically developing radiopharmaceutical market. Though many investors associate the company primarily with the commercial success of its prostate cancer imaging agent, Illuccix, I believe a closer look reveals a company building a multi-faceted theranostic platform with potentially huge, underappreciated long term value. Recent financial performance is great, but deliberate pipeline advancement, strategic infrastructure investments and key regulatory wins are what drive my bullish thesis right now. Telix is building its next fleet on top of its first success.

Strong Financials Fuel Investment

Telix recently reported financial results showing disciplined execution (all figures I talk about below are in $AUD). Revenue of $783.2 million is up 56 percent from $502.5 million in 2023. This performance, which surpassed full-year guidance, was largely driven by continued market penetration and sales of Illuccix. A second consecutive year of profitable growth is commendable, especially considering the significant period of strategic investment.

The company is banking profits but reinvesting them wisely to drive further expansion. The $194.6 million investment is in line with guidance and demonstrates a clear focus on late-stage assets - the very products meant to diversify revenue streams beyond Illuccix. Simultaneously, Telix is advancing its global supply chain and product delivery infrastructure. All this talk is accompanied by concrete actions such as the acquisitions of ARTMS, IsoTherapeutics Group, and RLS USA, and the expansion of its Brussels South facility. All these moves contributed an additional $15.8 million in manufacturing and distribution investment towards reliable, scaled production, including GMP production at the Brussels facility by 2025.

Operational leverage is showing. Adjusted EBITDA jumped by 70 percent to $99.3 million despite significant investments underway. The FY2025 revenue guidance of $1.18 billion to $1.23 billion ($770 million to $800 million) also signals confidence going forward. Specifically, this guidance considers established Illuccix revenue in authorized markets and the 11 months of contribution from the acquired RLS business (less any double-counting of RLS Illuccix revenue). What is absolutely excluded is revenue generated from products for which marketing authorization is not yet issued - Gozellix, Pixclara and Zircaix. Taking this conservative approach inevitably includes potential upside when these products reach regulatory milestones and enter the market. Combining that with guidance for an expanded R&D investment range (20% to 25% higher than FY2024), the financial picture is one of leveraging current success to aggressively fund the next generation of growth drivers.

Drug Pipeline

Telix's strategy is based on theranostics - the combination of diagnostic imaging agents and targeted therapeutic agents targeting the same biological marker. This integrated approach enables precise patient selection, treatment monitoring and possibly more effective therapies. Its pipeline reflects this with multiple targets and indications.

The Prostate Specific membrane antigen remains the cornerstone. It is the commercial success story of Illuccix that provides the financial engine. On top of that, there is another gallium-68 PSMA-11 agent TLX007-CDx, now FDA-approved Gozellix (with additional advantages discussed below). The last of the PSMA theranostic pairs is TLX591, an agent in late-stage (Phase 3) development via the ProstACT GLOBAL trial with expanded recruitment in the U.S. This shows a clear path from diagnosis to treatment in their lead franchise.

Beyond prostate cancer, Telix is meeting other critical unmet needs. The target for kidney cancer is Carbonic Anhydrase IX, with Zircaix being a Phase 3 diagnostic candidate along with TLX250, a Phase 2 therapeutic. This is similar to the PSMA strategy and aims at a total theranostic solution.

Brain cancer imaging and therapy target L-type amino acid Transporter 1 with Pixclara as the Phase 3 diagnostic candidate and TLX101 as the Phase 2 therapeutic approach.

A different CD66 target is available for different indications. The Phase 3 diagnostic TLX66-CDx/Scintimum targets musculoskeletal infections (osteomyelitis) and TLX66 is a Phase 2 therapeutic candidate for bone marrow conditioning in hematology.

The pipeline is not static. Telix said it has received clinical proof of concept for its first alpha therapy candidate in prostate cancer, TLX592, and is exploring next-generation isotopes potentially with higher efficacy. This acquisition, targeting the FAP5-targeting theranostic further demonstrates proactive business development in the pipeline, notably in the urological franchise. This demonstrates a multimodal strategy employing various isotopes and targeting modalities (small molecules, antibodies) for diverse high-need indications.

ZOLAR Trial Expansion

Recent news indicates that Telix is looking to expand its theranostic approach beyond its current boundaries. The announcement of the first patient dosed in the Phase 1 ZOLAR trial for TLX300-CDx in advanced, metastatic soft tissue sarcomas is important. Olaratumab, originally developed by Eli Lilly and Company, targets platelet-derived growth factor receptor alpha, a protein often over-expressed in STS. Telix holds exclusive worldwide rights to develop and commercialize radiolabelled forms.

The ZOLAR trial is a first-in-human, proof-of-concept PET study to establish the optimal dose for the diagnostic agent TLX300-CDx. But in keeping with Telix's theranostic philosophy, the study goes further. The dosimetry and target expression data collected in this imaging study should guide the selection of an appropriate therapeutic radionuclide for a future STS treatment candidate targeting PDGFR alpha. That move represents Telix's strategy to use diagnostic imaging to de-risk and guide the development of a paired therapeutic, potentially creating an entirely new oncology vertical for the company.

Gozellix FDA Approval Impact

The most dramatic recent development is Gozellix, Telix's next-generation PSMA-PET imaging agent for prostate cancer. Gozellix is radiolabeled with gallium-68 and targets the same patient populations as Illuccix - men with suspected metastasis candidates for first-definitive therapy and suspected recurrence based on increasing PSA levels.

It may seem redundant to launch another gallium-68 PSMA agent, but Gozellix has unique and commercially important advantages. Its novel formulation permits a shelf life of up to six hours and a wider distribution radius than other gallium-based imaging products. This isn't some technical tidbit; this is a market access unlocker. The ability to ship more reliably farther from the production site means Gozellix may be able to service PET imaging centers not accessible by any other imaging provider. It directly fills a critical unmet need by making accurate and useful advanced gallium-based imaging accessible to more patients in the United States. Moreover, the new formulation allows for more scalable production which may increase productivity, scheduling flexibility & throughput in busy scanning clinics.

Going forward, Gozellix hopes to build on Illuccix by offering choice to customers and patients. US PSMA-PET imaging has become the gold standard of care, but market penetration is still low - only a small fraction of the estimated 3.4 million men with prostate cancer have had this type of precision medicine scan. A key barrier is access. Telix feels that this is directly addressed in Gozellix. Further fueling this potential is the expectation that Gozellix will receive full reimbursement - possibly with low or no patient co-insurance. It could thus significantly enlarge its reach, especially among underserved patient populations - a real driver for growth that has not yet been reflected in the company's conservative forward guidance.

Risks and Competition

Clinical trial risk remains paramount; even with promising data, late-stage assets such as Zircaix, Pixclara, TLX591 and others must pass Phase 3 trials and regulatory approval. Delayed or negative trials are always possible. Commercialization risk also exists; launching new products such as Gozellix and achieving desired market uptake requires strong sales and marketing execution, as well as navigating complex reimbursement landscapes for future pipeline candidates.

Execution risk extends to operations. It will take some management to integrate the acquired businesses (ARTMS, IsoTherapeutics Group, RLS USA) and bring the expanded Brussels facility online for GMP production by 2025. Deficiencies or delays in scaling manufacturing could affect growth projections.

Radiopharmaceutical competition is becoming more intense. Establishment players such as Novartis (NVS) with Pluvicto and Locametz have resources, and numerous other companies are developing diagnostics and therapeutics targeting similar pathways, such as PSMA, so Telix has to differentiate with innovation, such as Gozellix's formulation or new targets like PDGFR alpha, efficient manufacturing, and strong commercial execution. Current revenue is heavily concentrated on Illuccix, so successful pipeline diversification is essential for long-term de-risking, though this takes time and capital.

Valuation and Market Size

Telix’s valuation, like many biotechs, is not accurately captured by conventional metrics. The potential of a radiopharma company lies not in trailing profits but in the size of its addressable markets. Let's take prostate cancer: with over 1.4 million new worldwide cases per year and about half eligible for advanced imaging, Telix targets around 700,000 patients a year. That equates to nearly $500 million in annual revenue potential at 25% penetration and $2,800 per scan - before you add Gozellix's wider reach or therapeutic follow-ons like TLX591.

The upside is substantial for kidney and brain cancers. Renal cell carcinoma accounts for 430,000 new global cases per year. If Telix applies its theranostic model here, it would have a market worth over $1 billion, especially for high-value therapeutic agents. Less common brain tumors like gliomas warrant premium pricing - treatment regimens with TLX101 could cost $20,000-$40,000 per patient. With the addition of CD66-targeted applications in bone marrow conditioning and infections, where treatment costs can exceed $50,000, the TAM reaches well beyond $10 billion.

Even modest market shares across these areas could outstrip revenues today. No, Telix does not need blockbuster hits - just steady approvals and smart execution to unlock multi-billion dollar opportunities.

Future Outlook

The risks aside, I think Telix Pharmaceuticals makes a compelling investment case. The company has demonstrated excellent commercial execution with Illuccix and generated substantial revenue and profit. More important, Telix is putting that success back into a deep, diversified pipeline based on theranostic principles. Recent FDA approval of Gozellix represents a validation and a near-term catalyst that could significantly broaden market access.

Its deliberate expansion of manufacturing and supply chain capabilities through acquisitions and facility build-outs demonstrates foresight and commitment to scaling operations globally. Several shots on goal are provided by the pipeline, including late-stage assets in key oncology indications and exploratory efforts in novel areas such as STS and alpha therapy.

Currently the financial guidance is conservative and excludes potential contributions from new approved or near approval products. If this pipeline matures and Gozellix picks up, I see strong opportunity for revenue diversification and continued strong growth. While competition and execution risks must be monitored, Telix's focused strategy, track record, and recent positive developments point to a strong future in the radiopharmaceutical space. If you have a long-term view and want exposure to this high-growth sector, Telix is worth considering.

data.title: New U.S. Tariffs: What It Means For Canada
data.description:
The U.S. has unveiled its reciprocal tariff plan. Derek Burleton, Deputy Chief Economist, TD looks at the potential impact for Canada’s economy, interest rates and the future of global trade

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The United States has unveiled a fresh round of tariffs impacting its global trading partners. And while Canada escaped those reciprocal tariffs, we're not totally unscathed. Joining us now to discuss is Derek Burleton, VP and Deputy Chief Economist with TD Economics. Derek, thanks so much for taking the time to talk with us.

Derek Burleton: My pleasure.

Greg Bonnell: So, as I said, we are watching, obviously, the developments in the Rose Garden live. We're saying, at first blush, like, where's Canada on that list? It seems we have escaped this latest round. But we already had some tariffs in place. What does it mean for the Canadian economy? Does it change our view of where we might be headed?

Derek Burleton: Well, yeah. Best deal in a bad deal. But I was braced for more tariffs yesterday. We didn't see them -- no net new. But we're still dealing with pre-existing tariffs -- steel and aluminum on the auto sector.

But I think even more, it's the uncertainty. We're hearing news that Chrysler is going to shut down for a couple of weeks in Windsor assembly, given the uncertainty around tariffs. So you're getting impacts. But for us, as forecasters, we had built a lot of that into our March forecast out a few weeks ago. We actually had a steeper tariff assumption than looks like it's going to happen, at least for now.

We had 12.5% average tariff rate on Canada. Based on yesterday's announcement, what we know, that's looking heavy. But then I still worry about further tariffs on copper and some of the other areas. So, for the most part, it looks like we avoided the worst. I worry about uncertainty impacts. It's certainly going to slow growth in Canada, and worried about potential escalation as well going forward.

Greg Bonnell: I know before yesterday as well that your team had done work on the potential government supports and what they could look like. Anything about what's happening right now that has changed your mind on that front?

Derek Burleton: Well, the two things I'm watching, I'm watching retaliation. And some of the early word we're getting on retaliation is it's going to be very targeted on the US auto sector from Canada's perspective. So we'll see how that rolls over in the United States, but hoping that's okay. But then that was expected.

But in terms of fiscal response, if anything, we will get quite an offset. You get an offset from a number of areas. You get an offset from lower interest rates. Canada's been very quick to cut rates.

You get an offset from a weak-ish Canadian dollar, which, actually, as of right now, is showing resilience. But we do have a soft Canadian dollar in our forecast. But as well, the fiscal response looks like it's going to exceed our expectations.

We had 1% of GDP, largely to support impacts from the tariffs. Provincially, we're already looking at that when you build in a likely Ontario response in its budget. Plus, you have all the election measures federally. And I think it's going to give the economy a bit of an offsetting lift. So, hopefully, we avoid kind of a significant recession in Canada. We might even get by with stagnation, even some mild growth this year.

Greg Bonnell: How much further can the Bank of Canada go? They have been delivering those rate cuts. We did have inflation back down to target. It's picked up a little bit. How much room do they have for the rest of this year to give more rate relief?

Derek Burleton: Well, they don't have a lot of room, but that's under a scenario where the economy doesn't encounter very, very steep headwinds, as we're not expecting at the moment. But all that to say, we had two cuts in our forecast.

This spring may be a bit delayed, because I think Bank of Canada is going to be looking at some upward inflation impact from Canadian tariffs on the US. They've got to walk the tightrope there with some of the weakening economy. I think, certainly, by the late spring, it's going to have enough evidence that the economy is weakening to cut rates on two more occasions.

And it'll stop there provided that, again, we avoid weaker downward scenarios that we're not currently embedding. So I think two cuts. That'll get short rates down to 2.25%, and I think should be enough to provide some of the underlying support to keep the economy from its head submerging too much.

Greg Bonnell: Even though we are in this tariff environment, of course, as goes the US economy, often goes the Canadian economy. When we take a look at the actions that have been taken by the Trump administration, what are your thoughts on the US economy?

Derek Burleton: Well, that it's weaker. But we had had an expectation for the US that was quite upbeat for this year. The trend was your friend there. The economy doing well quarter after quarter. We had assumed slowdown, but nothing too sharp -- something closer to trend 2%. Now with yesterday's announcement, we're certainly looking at a deep downgrade to that growth outlook -- at least a half a point.

Still positive for 2025, but 2026 could also get marked down. So certainly, a much weaker US forecast just based on deeper tariffs and uncertainty. And the job numbers tomorrow will be very key to give us a better sense of starting position. So we'll see what those numbers are like. But, overall, yes, we're looking at taking a cleaver to the US outlook in our next forecast.

And that won't help Canada. That's one of those, along with weaker commodity prices, you got to factor that into any Canadian equation. But with fiscal stimulus and the tariffs not being as steep as some had expected, I think there will be some offsets there.

Greg Bonnell: If the aim here of the US administration is to reshore, to bring manufacturing back to their country, what does that actually mean in terms of cost, timeline?

Derek Burleton: Well, I think the end goal is having the US supply its own needs as much as possible, and so a major shift, obviously, from the status quo -- several decades of globalization. How long that takes, I think there's certainly limits in the near term. You might get some increased capacity in the auto sector, for example. But, again, you can't just move over quickly. Auto plants are designed to supply certain vehicles. It would cost billions of dollars to retool. And then you've got labor shortages. And that's going to be, I think, longer term, a bit of an issue. So we'll see. I think the two- to three-year time frame is often given is when you can get significant investment response.

We, in our commentary, note that, by 2027, you could get 500,000 more jobs in US manufacturing, which, relative to the size of the economy, isn't massive. And the cost per job, given the cost of tariffs, would equate to about $1 million a job. So, given that robots and everything will be housed in a lot of those manufacturing facilities, there's certainly limits there.

Greg Bonnell: Before I let you go, Derek, I want to ask you, this reordering, really, of the global economy like you're talking about, what do we think about the global economy? Is there still a lot of dominoes to fall before we can figure out what the wide-ranging effects are?

Derek Burleton: Yes. We're talking from a very early stage. We've got to see retaliation. I worry about, for example, Europe retaliating on US services, intellectual property, digital service tax. That could take this trade war to a completely different level, given the US have threatened tit-for-tat responses to retaliations.

So a lot of potential headwinds there. Financial markets could be another headwind. So we have to sit back, monitor in the coming days. Hopefully, some cooler minds will prevail. And in a best case, you get trade deals with the US. You get a US administration that wants to shift to tax cuts, kind of the next stage of its agenda. And hopefully, we will see a number of deals fall in the coming weeks and months. And that'll kind of soothe a little bit of the nervousness right now.

Original Post

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - FOMC Minutes, Tariff Developments And Inflation Ahead
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review: Liberation day tariffs send markets tumbling

A bloodbath week for US Equity markets and markets as a whole has finally come to an end. Market sentiment soured after President Trump revealed his tariff proposals, which weighed on global growth expectations and sent US stocks tumbling.

On Thursday, hedge funds sold stocks at their highest single day amount since 2010. At the same time, retail investors bought $4.7 billion worth of stocks, the most in over a decade. Friday, the S&P 500 lost another $1.5 trillion in market value, bringing the total two-day losses to $3.5 trillion.

Friday's losses came about as China announced it will retaliate against U.S. President Donald Trump by imposing an extra 34% tariff on U.S. goods. This move intensifies the trade war, worrying investors and raising fears of a possible recession.

Source: LSEG, US Census Bureau

Developments this week saw investment bank J.P. Morgan increase their estimates to 60% that the global economy would enter a recession by year-end, up from 40% previously.

Big tech stocks dropped, pushing the Nasdaq closer to a bear market. Companies that rely heavily on China and Taiwan for manufacturing were hit hard, with Apple (AAPL) falling 6.4% and Nvidia (NVDA) dropping 7.7%.

The Nasdaq was set to close more than 20% below its record high from December.

It appears that market participants are now looking to the Federal Reserve and Chair Jerome Powell to be the saviour.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell told a business journalists' conference on Friday that the tariffs were "bigger than expected" and increased the risk of higher inflation and slower economic growth.

The Fed plans to wait for more data before deciding on monetary policy but will focus on keeping inflation under control if Trump's tariffs lead to lasting price increases, Powell said.

He didn’t directly comment on the U.S. stock market selloff but admitted that uncertainty is causing businesses to delay decisions. "People are just waiting for clarity," Powell said. "I can't say when it will clear up, but it eventually will."

On the FX front, The U.S. dollar strengthened against major currencies like the euro and yen on Friday after Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell discussed the impact of bigger-than-expected U.S. tariffs and took a cautious approach to future rate cuts.

The Aussie Dollar lost ground on Friday and hit five-year lows against the greenback after China announced its tariff stance and retaliation. The Swiss Franc and the Japanese Yen both benefitted this week from safe haven appeal, both gaining significant ground across the board.

Commodities were interesting to observe this week. Gold prices rose immediately after the tariff announcement but experienced a significant selloff since then. Gold reached a high of 3167 before the selloff saw prices touch a low of 3015 on Friday. The only explanation that I could draw was significant profit taking and the possibility that the tariffs were largely priced in.

Oil prices struggled this week as OPEC + announced further output increases. That coupled with tariff fears sent oil prices to their lowest level since 2021. Friday's losses were around 8%.

Heading into next week and the tariff picture is far from clear. Market participants will do well to focus on potential deals between the US and other countries, which could help sentiment improve. However, if no deals materialize and countries follow the China approach, markets could be in for another week of pain.

The week ahead: Markets eye tariff developments and the FOMC minutes

The upcoming week will focus on U.S. President Donald Trump's plans for new tariffs. Alongside this, markets will also watch U.S. jobs data, an Australian central bank meeting, and a key eurozone inflation report.

Asia Pacific Markets

The main focus this week in the Asia Pacific region will be Chinese inflation and Japanese wage data as the BoJ eyes a rate hike in the coming months.

Next week, China's low inflation could give the central bank (PBoC) room to cut interest rates. March inflation data, due Thursday, is expected to show consumer prices staying weak, with a slight rise of just 0.1% year-on-year.

Meanwhile, producer prices are likely to remain negative for the 30th straight month as input costs keep falling. These deflationary pressures and high real interest rates could push the PBoC to lower rates, though it has held off so far, waiting for the right moment.

The Bank of Japan will be watching wage data this week as the central bank eyes another rate hike. Wages are expected to improve gradually. February saw stronger labor cash earnings, helped by solid bonus payouts.

However, real cash earnings are likely to keep shrinking as inflation peaked in the same month. Inflation eased in March due to energy subsidies and lower fresh food prices, supporting the outlook for a steady rise in wages over time.

The Reserve Bank of New Zealand will announce its interest rate decision on Wednesday next week. Market participants are expecting the central bank to cut rates by 25 bps. Given the tariff developments and the backdrop of the New Zealand economy a 25 bps cut seems all but certain at this point.

Europe + UK + US

In developed markets, the US is the major data player next week, with the Eurozone and the UK enjoying a data reprieve.

The US economy is facing growing challenges as President Trump’s new trade policies spark concerns about higher prices and slower economic growth. Steep tariffs, which are paid by importing companies, could hurt profit margins and reduce consumer spending, especially as worries about job losses grow and stock markets decline.

While markets are increasingly expecting the Federal Reserve to cut interest rates, another high core CPI and PPI reading next week might make the Fed cautious about acting soon.

I will also be watching consumer confidence, which could drop further after recent events, and monitoring whether government spending cuts tied to DOGE are improving the fiscal situation.

The UK does not have any high impact data releases, but I will be keeping a close watch on Friday's GDP data print. After a slow January, I expect a small rebound in February's monthly GDP. These figures can be quite volatile, but despite a quieter end to 2024, the outlook for 2025 still seems positive, even with the recent tariff news.

There are still, of course, challenges, as evidenced by the Goldman Sachs group which lowered its UK GDP forecast down to 0.7% from a previous 0.8%.

The UK government is significantly increasing spending this year, which should help support growth. However, weaker economic activity in the US and eurozone could become a big challenge later this year and into 2026.

For all market-moving economic releases and events, see the MarketPulse Economic Calendar.

Chart of the week - Nasdaq 100

This week's focus shifts to the Nasdaq 100 which has now dropped over 20% from its all-time highs, signaling a bear market.

The Nasdaq is currently resting at an area of support around 17304 and finished the week with 3 successive days of losses.

The period-14 RSI is now resting in oversold territory, but as we know, the RSI can remain in oversold territory for extended periods of time, which means the bearish move may not be over.

This will also depend on how tariff developments shake up next week. Immediate support rests at 17000 before the 16500 and 16000 handle comes into focus. Resistance rests at around 17737 before the 18361 and 18852 handles come into focus.

Nasdaq 100 Daily Chart - April 4, 2025

Source: TradingView.com

Key Levels to Consider:

Support

  • 17000
  • 16500
  • 16000

Resistance

  • 17737
  • 18361
  • 18852

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Palantir: The Value Layer Hidden In Plain Sight
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

There's a strange phenomenon in investing where some companies are so widely discussed that they become misunderstood. Palantir (PLTR) could be one of them.

It's the company investors are supposed to love—because it's AI, because it's defense, because it's mysterious—but ask a room of smart people what exactly Palantir does, and you'll get metaphors instead of business models. "It's like the CIA, but for data." "It's Excel on steroids." "It's an AI operating system." Not wrong, but not actionable.

This is a problem, especially because Palantir may be building one of the most valuable technology positions in the modern enterprise: the AI-powered decision layer.

And the place to understand that? Not Silicon Valley. Japan.

The SOMPO Blueprint—Operationalizing Intelligence

In 2019, Palantir and SOMPO Holdings (OTCPK:SMPNY)(OTCPK:NHOLF) co-founded Palantir Japan, a dedicated joint venture aimed at helping Japanese institutions modernize through data integration and operational AI.1

The premise wasn't flashy. SOMPO, one of Japan's largest insurers and long-term care providers, needed to digitize legacy workflows and respond to an aging population. But this wasn't about migrating to the cloud or buying an analytics dashboard. This was about building new feedback loops—where frontline workers, data models and strategic decisions all operate in sync.

  • Care Business: SOMPO operates 1,100 facilities across 25 prefectures, serving approximately 85,000 care recipients.2 Foundry helps generate personalized care plans, track interventions and coordinate with family members—automating regulatory reporting in the process.
  • Commercial Insurance: In its core business, Palantir's tools enable more than 10,000 insurance agents to dynamically price, quote and flag risks with contextual intelligence baked into their workflows.3
  • Disaster Response: In a country exposed to frequent natural disasters, SOMPO leveraged Palantir to pre-position resources and streamline claims processing when crises strike.

By 2023, SOMPO had signed a $50 million expansion,4 deploying Palantir across its group companies. What started as a partnership had become infrastructure. They weren't buying software—they were embedding a decision layer into the enterprise.

Beyond Dashboards—Why Palantir Is Not Just SaaS

Most enterprise software helps you see what's already happening. Palantir helps you act on what hasn't yet happened.

That's because its core platforms—Foundry and AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform)—don't stop at analytics. They allow organizations to encode logic, constraints, domain knowledge and even regulatory rules into dynamic workflows.

For example:5

  • A traditional insurer uses a rules engine to assess risk tiers. Palantir lets agents run simulations on millions of risk inputs live—flagging anomalies and surfacing recommended actions.
  • A hospital using standard software can visualize ICU capacity. With Palantir, it can simulate future outbreaks, allocate resources proactively and automate compliance reporting to regulators—all in one loop.

This distinction—between reporting tools and orchestration layers—is subtle but profound. Palantir doesn't sit on top of operations. It becomes part of them.

Another thing that caught our attention—SOMPO is a diversified financial firm in Japan with a focus on insurance. We just wrote about how Warren Buffett's strategic investments in Japan's five largest trading houses—ITOCHU, Marubeni, Mitsubishi, Mitsui and Sumitomo—highlight his recognition of their diversified business models and attractive valuations.

SOMPO Holdings shares similarities with these trading houses, particularly in its diversification and strategic partnerships. As one of Japan's leading insurance companies, SOMPO has expanded beyond traditional insurance services into areas like health care and digital transformation. We covered a notable example: its collaboration with Palantir Technologies to enhance data analytics and operational efficiency.

We also couldn't help but note that SOMPO had a P/E ratio below 10.0x as of the middle of March 2025, which is another point of similarity to the five Japanese trading houses that were featured in our article about Buffett's holdings.6

Global Case Study—Signals of Strategic Depth with Important European Aerospace Company

Airbus (OTCPK:EADSF): Industrial Digital Twin at Scale

Europe's aerospace giant adopted Palantir to power its Skywise platform,7 integrating data from more than 11,700 aircraft in a manner that more than 44,000 users worldwide can take advantage of. Some 30+ apps and datasets are available, allowing airlines to use Palantir to predict component failures, optimize fuel usage and reroute logistics in real time. This isn't about dashboards; it's about flying safer, cheaper and smarter.8

Why It's So Hard to Value—and Why That Might Be the Opportunity

Investors often struggle with Palantir's valuation not because it's overhyped, but because it's hard to categorize. It doesn't fit cleanly into software as a service (SaaS). It's not a cloud company. Its government revenue adds another layer of cognitive noise.

But here's a contrarian lens: Palantir may be building the middleware monopoly of the AI era.

Just as Microsoft became indispensable in the personal computer (PC) age by owning the operating system (OS) layer, Palantir could become indispensable in the age of autonomous enterprises by owning the decision-making layer. The company's software doesn't just analyze data—it captures context. It adapts workflows. It learns institutional behavior.

That's not just sticky; it's systemic.

The Investor Takeaway—Follow the Embeddedness

If you think of Palantir as a vendor, you'll struggle to justify the multiple. But if you see it as an operating substrate, a radically different picture emerges.

The most important metric for Palantir might not be annual recurring revenue (ARR) or contract wins. It might be its depth of integration—how far into the enterprise its logic extends.

This is what makes SOMPO the most underappreciated case study in the Palantir story. Not because it's flashy, but because it shows what "embedded AI infrastructure" actually looks like.

Palantir doesn't win by selling software licenses. It wins when it becomes the nervous system of an institution.

Conclusion: Palantir Is Not Selling AI—It's Selling Institutional Agility

The next decade won't be won by companies that adopt AI tools. It'll be won by companies that rebuild around AI thinking.

That's what SOMPO did. That's what Palantir enables.

And that's why this company, however messy the narrative, may be one of the few selling real, repeatable, scalable intelligence.

Not because it's AI. But because it's real.

Footnotes

    1. Source: PRESS RELEASE: SOMPO and Palantir Co-Found Palantir Japan, November 18, 2019
    2. Source: SOMPO Holdings, Inc. (2024). Integrated Report 2024. Tokyo, Japan
    3. Source: Palantir Signs $50 Million Expansion with SOMPO Holdings
    4. Source: Palantir Signs $50 Million Expansion with SOMPO Holdings
    5. Source: Palantir for Insurance
    6. Sources: Yahoo Finance for SOMPO's P/E ratio as of 3/14/25; the following article reported the P/E ratios of the five trading houses as of March 14, 2025
    7. Source: Airbus launches Skywise - aviation's open data platform
    8. Source: Skywise Core

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Central Banks Keep Gold In Focus In February
data.description:
Demand for gold from central bankers continues, with data available for February showing reported global central bank gold reserves rising by 24t. Thus far, Poland, China, Turkey and the Czech Republic have led gold demand from emerging market central banks.

Chart 1: Official gold reserves rose by a further 24t in February

Monthly reported central bank activity, tonnes*

  • National Bank of Poland (NBP) lead month and year-to-date net buying, adding 29t in February alone. Year-to-date, NBP added 32t of gold, with its total gold holdings now 480t or 20% of its total reserves
  • People’s Bank of China (PBoC) added 5t of gold in February, its fourth consecutive month of net buying since it resumed reporting in November 2024
  • The Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey added 3t of gold in February, with its gold reserves totalling 623t or 38% of its total reserves
  • The Central Bank of Jordan also added 3t of gold in February, with total gold reserves now at 72t or 30% of total reserves
  • Qatar Central Bank reported 2t of net buying in February; its total gold holdings now stand at 114t, which constitutes 19% of total reserves
  • The Czech National Bank added 2t of gold to their reserves this month, its gold holdings stand at 55t or 3% of total reserves
  • Net sellers for the month of February were the National Bank of Kazakhstan (-8t) and the Central Bank of Uzbekistan (-12t). Both central banks lead net selling activity year-to-date, down 4t
  • Despite net sales y-t-d, gold reserves form 54% of Kazakhstan's total reserves, bringing its total to 280t.

The National Bank of Belgium (NBB) has addressed recent media reports on the monetisation of Belgium’s gold reserve for public finance purposes, particularly defence spending. In a recent press release, NBB reaffirmed its independence and said gold reserves constitute assets allocated for achieving “public interest tasks entrusted to the Bank.”

Chart 2: Central banks' appetite for gold continues in February 2025

Y-t-d central bank net purchases and sales*

Original Post

data.title: Multi-Asset Income In A New Regime
data.description:
By Karen Watkin, CFA & Fahd Malik

Transcript

Karen Watkin: Generating income is often in front of mind for many investors, but the market conditions today are very different from those that we’ve had over the past decade or so. So taking a flexible approach to income investing is critical.

Fahd Malik: So we focus on efficient income, and the way I would describe efficient income is focusing on income that comes from assets that have good risk-adjusted returns. And in this environment, you don’t really have to reach down in quality to generate efficient income. You can do so by buying higher-quality assets and positioning defensively. But what’s important to keep in mind in this environment is having a global multi-sector approach. You got to look for opportunities outside of the US and not just that, across sectors as well. So the places where we find attractive opportunities right now are emerging-market corporate debt, European high yield and securitized assets.

Karen Watkin: When we saw bond yields languishing towards the historical end of their ranges, it was important for investors that wanted a compelling level of income to be able to look much further afield. Your fixed-income assets can give you really attractive sources of yield, and so now it’s about being able to pivot your portfolio to think about the role that the other asset classes and strategies can bring to you. So for example, if you think about your equities, even just a few years ago, the gap between your dividend yields and high-yield credit bonds was not very much. It was less than a percent. If you fast-forward to today, actually that gap has now widened to almost 4%. So we think it makes sense to actually be far more balanced with your equities. Yes, have some of those high-dividend payers, but don’t just put all your eggs in one basket. Include some of those secular winners that can benefit from trends like AI that may not be paying much in dividends but actually give you really great upside potential.

Fahd Malik: What’s also important to remember is that policy might diverge across the globe. And what that means is you got to be nimble, not just in the overall level of duration, but in which country you take that duration in. We are matching the growth risk of a country with its duration risk. So, for instance, if you are taking credit and equity risk in the US, we are buying US duration because we think that leads to a better outcome for clients.

Karen Watkin: Taking a multi-asset approach to income investing can provide a really interesting opportunity for investors because it enables them to capture that growth alongside the income that they want to generate. It also means by having a well-diversified portfolio, that they should be able to experience a smoother path of returns, as well as getting access to a broader array of income sources.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BST: A Technology CEF Under Pressure, A Long Way To Go
data.description:
Thesis

We have not covered the BlackRock Science and Technology Trust (NYSE:BST) in over two years. The name is a closed-end fund focused on technology equities, and represents a structural wrapper to extract dividends from technology stocks. In our last article, we highlighted the CEF's structural premium versus a background of valuation ratios for its underlying components.

After the significant market events from the first week of April 2025 which saw widespread selling on the back of new Trump tariffs, we are going to re-visit the fund. We will highlight for investors the best way to think about BST, and why the fund has a significant way to go until it can be considered 'cheap'. While we think the market is currently oversold, and we might see a near term bounce, longer term we are of the opinion that technology equities will be under pressure, thus translating into a weak BST forward.

A CEF is a structural wrapper. BST is a take on tech equities.

If one ignores the word CEF and just uses the word fund here, the picture becomes more clear. BST is a fund that holds tech equities:

The fund is overweight semiconductors, software and tech hardware. Its top ten names are very well known tech giants:

BST is structured as a CEF so that it can structurally provide for a high dividend yield and return of capital when needed, all within the liquidity constraints of a closed end fund. There is no 'magic' when it comes to a CEF wrapper versus a pure ETF when returns are concerned. Both structures will derive gains from the underlying equities moving up in value. Conversely, when the market sells-off, both types of instruments will face significant losses.

One aspect which is very specific to CEFs however is the premium/discount to NAV. We are going to discuss this aspect in the next section, while we will analyze the valuation background for the sector in the subsequent one.

Discount to NAV - a long way to go

While BlackRock has been very proactive in addressing chronic discounts to NAV for its CEF family via tender offers, CEFs do have a high beta to the underlying asset class:

As we can notice from the above chart, the last time tech came under pressure in 2022, the CEF saw a massive move in its premium to NAV from a +10% figure to a -5% level. This time is not going to be any different. The actions taken by BlackRock are meant to address chronic CEF issues, not wrong way market bets. If tech continues to lead on the way down, do expect BST to move to a -5% to -10% discount to NAV, consistent with its historic ranges.

Valuation concern for tech

Tech has led the way down this year, after a tremendous bull market that saw many names double in value:

As we can see from the above chart, BST has been closely tracking the Nasdaq this year, an index which is represented by the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ) fund in the above chart. We have also introduced the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLK) in the above comparison.

Kindly note, we are using a total return metric above in order to capture the idiosyncrasies of the CEF structure, which tends to distribute gains via high-interest proceeds. The underlying risk factors are otherwise quite similar. We can observe this correlation historically as well:

While BST underperformed since the 2022 drawdown, it did much better than QQQ or XLK during the 2020/2021 bull market. The correlations are very strong, with the funds exhibiting similar compositions.

We have warned investors regarding tech and the AI theme repeatedly:

  • ROM: De-Risking Means De-Leveraging In Today's Environment: the fund is now down -20% since our 'Sell' rating
  • AIPI: Laggard In The AI Space, Unsupported Distribution: the fund is down -14% since our 'Sell' rating

The common thread in these article were the sky high starting valuations for the tech sector:

With starting P/E ratios of 28x, tech was at the beginning of the year in an astounding 87% 10-year percentile. Make no mistake about it, while it might take time, financial markets always engage in mean reversion. And usually, mean reversion also shows a propensity to overshoot to the downside.

While the last weeks have seen a decrease in P/E ratios, we are far, far away from long term fair values. Will the market just continue its way down and have a -30% drawdown this month? We think not. Even bear markets do not work that way. For any severe drawdown like the recent one, we will have some sort of re-bound once the tariff picture clears up a bit and the market is more comfortable with the overall macro. However, long term, tech will be under pressure, and given its composition BST will be too.

Distribution coverage

While the current distribution is well covered, expect changes:

Please note that the March 2025 distribution comes entirely from realized long-term gains. This purely means the CEF sold at a gain some of the holdings in its portfolio. As those gains move to losses, the fund will not have any further gains, and will thus rely on return of capital. We are not there yet, but a suddenly decreasing NAV will eventually mean zero capital gains.

Why not a Sell rating then?

The recent move has been extremely violent, with April 3, 2025 recording the largest one day move down in tech since the 2020 Covid days. This is not normal, and the markets are left severely oversold. We are due for a bounce here. The magnitude is debatable, but it will happen. Long term BST holders can make a decision after a small bounce regarding net desired position, while new entrants should think twice regarding buying into an asset class which is structurally moving lower to re-set hyperinflated valuations.

Conclusion

BST is a technology equity CEF. The name has a very high correlation to the Nasdaq, and simply represents a way to extract dividends from the tech equity asset class via the CEF wrapper. We are of the opinion that tech has a significant way down in order to re-align valuations with historic norms. Further to that BST structurally will see its discount to NAV widen, as it has done in all tech bear markets. Therefore, there is significant down pressure on the fund long term. However, on a shorter time-frame we expect a small bounce after a vicious sell-off, the largest seen since the 2020 Covid days. We are therefore a 'Hold' here, but caution new entrants to stay away, while holders might want to re-think position size after the expected bounce.

data.title: Ames National: Reducing The Earnings Estimate Following Tariff Announcements And Downgrading To Hold
data.description:
Earnings of Ames National Corporation (NASDAQ:ATLO) will likely decline this year because of tariffs and their impact on inflation and credit risk. Further, the tariffs have dampened the outlook for commercial and agricultural loan growth. On the other hand, continued margin expansion will likely lift the bottom line. The average loan yield will continue to expand as low-yielding fixed-rate loans mature. At the same time, the average deposit cost will continue to dip because of interest rate cuts. Overall, I’m expecting Ames National’s EPS to dip by 2.6% to $1.11 per share in 2025. Compared to my last report written in January 2025, I’ve reduced my EPS estimate because I’ve increased my operating expenses estimate and decreased my loan growth estimate following the most recent tariff announcements. The year-end target price is below the current market price. Based on the total expected return, I’m downgrading Ames National to a hold rating.

Loan Outlook has Sharply Deteriorated Due to Tariffs

As per the latest news reports, the U.S. Government has levied fresh tariffs on most countries of the world. However, there is a chance that the U.S. Government will roll back, negotiate down, or delay some of these new tariffs as it did last month with Canada and Mexico. There is a lot of uncertainty in that regard. However, one thing is for sure, the operating environment for most American businesses will not be conducive to investments and decision-making. Businesses need stable policies to make capital expenditure decisions. Hence, the uncertain trade policy will erode the demand for commercial loans.

The PMI indices were already showing a slowdown last month; the new tariffs should result in even more slowdown. The manufacturing PMI index worsened last month, while the services PMI index dropped close to the contractionary territory, which is below 50.

Ames National’s loan growth was already quite low last year, coming in at just 2.0% for the full year, which was below the four-year compounded annual growth of 5.1% from 2020 to 2024. Due to the outlook on commercial loans, I think 2025 will also be a year of low growth.

Apart from Commercial Loans, Ames National also focuses on residential real estate loans, which make up almost a quarter of total loans.

The company lends in the state of Iowa through its six subsidiary banks. The environment is currently not conducive to residential loan growth in that state because, firstly, mortgage rates are stubbornly high despite the 100-basis points rate cuts late last year. Secondly, Iowa’s house price index got steeper in late 2024, which is bad for housing affordability and consequently new mortgage demand.

Agriculture is another key lending area for Ames National. Unfortunately, Iowa’s agricultural segment is highly dependent on exports, which isn’t good in the ongoing trade conflict. According to the U.S. Trade Representative, Iowa is the country’s second-largest agricultural exporting state. Even more unfortunate is the fact that Iowa’s largest market is Canada, which represents 30% of the state’s total goods exports. If the Liberal Party of Canada returns to power in the elections later this month, then we can expect Canada’s commitment to retaliating to the U.S. government’s tariffs to continue.

Overall, I’m expecting the loan portfolio to grow by just 1% in 2025. Further, I’m expecting deposit growth to match loan growth. In my last report on the company, I had projected a loan growth of 4.6% for this year. My outlook has worsened considerably since the last report because of the tariffs.

Margin to Benefit from Declining Deposit Costs, Rising Loan Yields

Ames National’s net interest margin shot up by 17 basis points in the last quarter to stand at 2.38%. The growth beat my already optimistic estimate given in my last report on the company. As I had anticipated in my report, the average deposit cost declined due to the Fed funds rate cuts, while the average loan yield surged as low-yielding loans matured. Many of these maturing loans were fixed-rate, and they had been originated before the last 525 basis points up-rate cycle, so they carried very low yields. Despite the 100-basis points rate cuts of last year, the proceeds from the maturing loans were reinvested at higher yields.

For the first quarter of the year, the following two points must affect the net interest margin.

  1. The average deposit cost must stabilize as there were no Fed funds rate cuts.
  2. The average loan yield must continue to expand as fixed-rate loans mature.

As per detail given in the 10-K filing, fixed-rate and variable-rate loans maturing this year made up 21% of total loans at the end of December 2024.

For the remaining three quarters of 2025, I’m expecting the loan yields to continue to climb as low-yielding fixed-rate loans mature. I’m also expecting the average deposit cost to dip a bit because I’m still expecting a 50-basis points rate cut in 2025 despite the new tariffs that should boost inflation. I think the Fed will feel pressured to cut rates to improve the economic slowdown; therefore, I’ve decided to not change my interest rate outlook for 2025.

Overall, I’m expecting the net interest margin to grow by 9 basis points in 2025 from the last quarter of 2024. In my last report as well, I was expecting the margin to grow by 9 basis points from 4Q-2024. However, my average margin estimate is higher now because the fourth quarter’s margin growth exceeded my expectations. At the time of the last report, I was expecting an average net interest margin of 2.3% for 2025, whereas now I’m expecting an average margin of 2.4%.

Reducing the Earnings Estimate Because of Tariffs

Ames National’s provision expense for loan losses, non-interest income, and non-interest expenses for 2024 were all in line with my expectations given in my last report on the company. However, the actual earnings per share beat my expectations because the net interest margin was higher than I had anticipated. I had projected an EPS of $1.02 while the actual EPS reported was $1.14 for 2024.

For 2025, I’m expecting the margin growth to support earnings. Further, the positive, albeit low, loan growth will lend some support to the bottom line. However, because of the tariffs, I’m expecting inflation to drive up non-interest expenses, which will hurt earnings. I’m expecting the efficiency ratio to rise to 77.2% in 2025 from 76.6% in 2024. Compared to my last report, my outlook for non-interest expenses is worse now because of the tariffs and their impact on inflation.

Additionally, I’m assuming that the provision expense for loan losses will rise this year because there is now a greater risk of default, especially the default of loans taken by agricultural exporters. I’m expecting the provision expense to make up 0.12% of loans in 2025, up from 0.05% in 2024, but still below the 2020 level of 0.50%.

I’m also making the assumption that the non-interest income will remain stable.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report earnings of $1.11 per share for 2025, down by 2.6% year-over-year. In my last report, I projected an EPS of $1.19 per share for 2025. I’ve reduced my estimate because I’ve increased my non-interest expense estimate and reduced my loan growth estimate for the year.

Downgrading to Hold

In my last report on Ames National written in January 2025, I adopted a buy rating with a December 2025 target price of $18.7. The stock price has grown by ~0.4% since my report (including the 5% plunge on April 3 due to the tariffs), thereby outperforming the market.

I usually use the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book value (“P/TB”) multiples to value banking stocks. However, because the tariffs have affected the entire market, I think the valuation multiples assigned by the market to stocks can remain discounted to historical multiples for now. I think it’s better to use peer multiples.

ATLO MRBK HNVR CZWI CBFV Peer Average
P/E GAAP ("ttm") 14.57 9.02 11.81 10.46 11.45 10.69
P/E GAAP ("fwd") - 7.60 8.43 10.78 15.08 10.47
P/B ("ttm") 0.85 0.85 0.73 0.78 0.95 0.83
P/TB ("ttm") 0.92 0.87 0.81 0.95 1.02 0.91

Source: Seeking Alpha

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with my EPS forecast gives a target price of $11.8 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with my EPS forecast gives a target price of $16.6 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $14.2, which implies a 14.0% downside from the April 3 closing price of $16.56.

Ames National also offers a dividend yield of 4.8% at the current quarterly dividend rate of $0.20 per share. Adding the price downside to the dividend yield gives a total expected return of negative 9.2%. As this expected return isn’t bad enough for a sell rating, I’m going to downgrade Ames National to a hold rating.
